summary:
The Stonewall Riots were a series of spontaneous uprisings by members of the LGBT community in response to a police raid on the Stonewall Inn, a gay bar in Greenwich Village, New York City, in the early hours of June 28, 1969. Police raids on gay bars were routine in the 1960s, as same-sex intimacy was illegal in most American states and police regularly arrested, harassed, and extorted patrons of LGBT establishments. On this night, patrons and bystanders resisted arrest, and the confrontation escalated into several nights of protests and rioting around the bar.

The Stonewall uprising catalyzed the modern LGBT rights movement in the United States and internationally. Within months, gay liberation organizations had been formed across the country, adopting a more confrontational stance than the earlier homophile movement's approach of respectability and gradual legal reform. The first gay pride marches were held in New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Chicago in June 1970 to mark the first anniversary of Stonewall, establishing an annual tradition that spread globally.

Stonewall is now widely recognized as the pivotal moment when the LGBT rights movement transformed into a mass political movement demanding equality rather than tolerance. Over the following decades, the movement achieved significant legal progress including the decriminalization of homosexuality in most Western countries, anti-discrimination protections, and ultimately the legalization of same-sex marriage in many nations. The Stonewall Inn was designated a National Monument in 2016.
